title: Who uses cohort tools and why it matters
body:
Healthcare data analysts define patient populations for research and clinical analysis.

Cohort building is the no-code way to group patients by disease, demographics, enrollment, and more.

title: The activation problem
body:
Legacy tool (Prism): configure → submit → wait 30–60 min → report.

New users depended on account managers for ~3 months before independent use. Most people didn’t build cohorts daily — they relied on AMs as a crutch.

This wasn’t just a UX problem. It was a business model problem: you can’t scale if every new user needs a human guide.

title: We started with the foundation problem
body:
Users couldn’t see what they were building in real time.

No feedback loop meant building blind, low confidence, and account-manager dependency.

What does “confident” look like for a user building cohorts?

We ran user interviews and usability sessions on the existing flow.

title: Designing real-time validation
body:
Aggregate panels: counts and visualizations as you build.

Customizable panels — cohort-building has multiple use cases.

Early prototypes, tested with analysts — use the carousel for 2–3 iteration screens.

Key decision: what aggregates to surface by default vs. what to make accessible but not default.

title: Then we added AI — and found the wrong assumption
body:
Same users, same cohort problem. They didn’t trust the AI output.

We followed the standard agentic pattern: chat-first.

Assumption baked in: users would keep chatting with the AI throughout the session.

Research showed: they didn’t. One prompt, then back to point-and-click — about three weeks building in the wrong direction.

title: The behavioral insight that changed everything
body:
Actual usage: analysts moved between familiar tools and occasional AI help.

They wanted AI within reach — not AI owning the experience.

User voice (paraphrased): “I’m not trying to live in chat — I need the builder.”

Reframe: the winning layout should match how people actually work, not how we assumed they would.

title: The new layout — AI alongside, not in charge
body:
New layout, familiar and comfortable.

AI: accessible and reachable, not dominant.

title: Designing for trust — the transparency problem
body:
The design decision: show **just enough** for people to understand and work with the system.

What we implemented:

1. **Progressive disclosure** — by design.
2. **Surface just enough**, and make everything else easily accessible.
3. **User control** — make it clear everything is modifiable; they can pause or delete anything at any point.

title: Engineering in the room from day one
body:
Not a handoff — engineers in research sessions.

They knew why, not only what.

Prototyped with Figma Make for rapid live testing — faster feedback loops.

When research changed direction (week 3 pivot), engineering moved with us because they understood the why.

title: From 3 months to 90 seconds
body: New-user activation: ~3 months of AM dependency → about 90 seconds self-serve for key flows.

prototypeCta.label: check out the vibe coded prototype ;)

title: The research shaped what came next
body:
Real-time aggregate panels became reusable infrastructure — carried directly into subsequent product work.

AI transparency patterns (surface just enough, make the rest accessible) became a design principle applied across the platform.

The “AI alongside, not owning” layout principle influenced how the team thought about AI placement in other features.

Codeset architecture introduced here became a foundational component reused in code search and clinical code discovery features.

title: What this taught me — and why it matters here

learningColumns.title: Existing patterns carry hidden assumptions. Name them before you build on them.
learningColumns.body: Every pattern carries a hidden assumption. We named ours about three weeks too late — chat-first assumed ongoing conversation; analysts wanted one prompt, then the builder.

learningColumns.title: AI confidence is a product problem, not a feature problem.
learningColumns.body: Trust is designed, not assumed: surface just enough to move confidently; put the rest one deliberate step away.

learningColumns.title: Frequency of updates isn’t alignment. Documentation is.
learningColumns.body: Leadership communication lesson: coverage and a paper trail beat stand-ups — so decisions travel when the room changes.
